column4: Researcher 1 treated the two stories as variants of publishing visibility, not as a strict contradiction.
column5: Researcher 2 treated “few people” and “everyone publicly” as incompatible access-control goals.
column8: The final label is CONFLICT because the same publishing action has mutually exclusive visibility intentions: limited sharing versus public sharing.
column9: As a Publisher, I want to publish a dataset, so that I can view just the dataset with a few people.
column10: As a Publisher, I want to publish a dataset, so that I can share the dataset publicly with everyone.

column4: Researcher 1 treated “dataset” and “data package” as different artifacts and therefore did not assign a direct dependency.
column5: Researcher 2 treated client configuration as a prerequisite for publishing data packages or datasets.
column8: The final label is DEPENDS_ON because publishing requires the client to be configured first at the requirements level.
column9: As a Publisher, I want to publish a dataset, so that I can share the dataset publicly with everyone.
column10: As a Publisher, I want to configure my client, so that I can start publishing data packages.

column4: Researcher 1 treated notification of changes and knowing what changed as part of the same change-awareness requirement.
column5: Researcher 2 treated notification as a trigger that enables the consumer to inspect detailed changes afterward.
column8: The final label is DUPLICATE because both stories focus on consumer awareness of package changes and taking action based on those changes.
column9: As a Consumer, I want to know full details of how the data package schema has changed, so that that I can adjust my scripts to handle it.
column10: As a Consumer, I want to be notified of changes to a package I care about, so that that I can check out what has changed and take action.

column4: Researcher 1 treated importing and updating as separate publishing operations.
column5: Researcher 2treated updating an already registered package as dependent on the package first being imported into the registry.
column8: Researcher 2interpreted importing into the registry as a prerequisite for later tagging.
column9: As a Publisher, I want to import my data package into the registry, so that my data has a permanent online home to access.
column10: As a Publisher, I want to use a publish command to update a data package that is already in the registry, so that it appears there.

column4: Researcher 1 treated importing and tagging as separate registry features.
column5: Researcher 2treated tagging or snapshotting as requiring that the data package already exists on the registry server.
column8: The final label is DEPENDS_ON because creating a snapshot or tag on the registry requires the package to be present in the registry first.
column9: As a Publisher, I want to import my data package into the registry, so that my data has a permanent online home to access.
column10: As a Publisher, I want to tag datapackage to create a snapshot of data on the registry server, so that that consumers can refer to it.

column4: Researcher 1 reasoned that consumers can only search packages after publishers configure clients and publish packages.
column5: Researcher 2treated publisher-side configuration and consumer-side search as different workflows with no direct dependency.
column8: The final label is NONE because configuring a publisher client is not directly required by the consumer search requirement.
column9: As a Publisher, I want to configure my client, so that I can start publishing data packages.
column10: As a Consumer, I want to search data packages, so that that I can find the ones I want.

column4: Researcher 1 connected publisher configuration to making publisher-owned packages searchable.
column5: Researcher 2treated configuration and searching within a publisher’s packages as separate functions.
column8: The final label is NONE because searching among a publisher’s packages does not directly depend on the client-configuration story.
column9: As a Publisher, I want to configure my client, so that I can start publishing data packages.
column10: As a Consumer, I want to search among all data packages owned by a publisher, so that that I can easily find one data package amongst all the data packages by this publisher.

column4: Researcher 1 linked publisher updates with the analyst’s ability to obtain the latest version.
column5: Researcher 2treated server-side publishing and local analyst updates as separate user goals.
column8: The final label is NONE because updating a registry package and updating a previously downloaded local package are related by topic but are not the same requirement and do not form a direct dependency.
column9: As a Publisher, I want to use a publish command to update a data package that is already in the registry, so that it appears there.
column10: As a Data Analyst, I want to update previously downloaded data package, so that that I can work with the most recent data.

column4: Researcher 1 interpreted publishing in the UI as a prerequisite for later displaying the package attractively.
column5: Researcher 2treated both stories as concerning publication and public presentation of the data package.
column8: The final label is DUPLICATE because both stories express the goal of making a published data package available and visible to consumers.
column9: As a Publisher, I want to publish a data package in the UI, so that that it is available and published.
column10: As a publisher, I want to show the world how my published data is, so that that it immediately catches consumer's attention.

column4: Researcher 1 treated both stories as previewing a prepared data package before sharing or publishing.
column5: Researcher 2focused on the CLI and view-configuration details and therefore treated the first story as more specific than generic previewing.
column8: The final label is DUPLICATE because both stories describe previewing a prepared data package to check or refine it before publication or sharing.
column9: As a Publisher, I want to be able to preview the views of the current data package using cli prior to publishing, so that that I can refine the json declarations of datapackage view section to achieve a great looking result.
column10: As a Publisher, I want to preview a datapackage I have prepared, so that that I can check it works and share the results.

column4: Researcher 1 treated viewing a data package online and viewing a specific version online as the same general viewing requirement.
column5: Researcher 2distinguished generic online viewing from version-specific online viewing.
column8: The final label is DUPLICATE because the second story is a more specific formulation of the same core capability: viewing a data package online.
column9: As a Consumer, I want to view a data package online, so that I can get a sense of whether this is the dataset I want.
column10: As a Consumer, I want to view a Datapackage at a particular version online, so that that I can present/discuss the particular data timeslice of interest with other people.

column4: Researcher 1 treated package presentation and publisher profile viewing as separate consumer-facing features.
column5: Researcher 2treated the publisher profile as supporting discovery and evaluation of published data.
column8: The final label is DEPENDS_ON because presenting published data to consumers is supported by the publisher profile context, which helps users discover and assess packages.
column9: As a publisher, I want to show the world how my published data is, so that that it immediately catches consumer's attention.
column10: As a Consumer, I want to see a publisher's profile, so that that I can discover their packages and get a sense of how active and good they are.

column4: Researcher 1 distinguished publisher-oriented examples for learning the format from consumer-oriented examples for exploring the site.
column5: Researcher 2treated both stories as requesting quick access to example published packages.
column8: The final label is NONE because the actors and purposes differ: one supports publishers learning how to publish, while the other supports consumers evaluating site usefulness.
column9: As a Publisher, I want to see real examples of published packages so that I can understand how useful and simple the datapackage format and the registry is.
column10: As a Consumer, I want to see some example data packages quickly, so that I get a sense of what is on this site and if it is useful to look further.

column4: Researcher 1 treated both stories as search requirements over data packages, where description-based search and publisher-based search are variants of the same search functionality.
column5: Researcher 2distinguished the search criteria: one story concerns keyword or description-based search, while the other concerns filtering packages by publisher ownership.
column8: The final label is NONE because searching by package description and searching within one publisher’s packages use different criteria and express separate requirements.
column9: As a Consumer, I want to search based on description of data package, so that that I can find package which related to some key words.
column10: As a Consumer, I want to search among all data packages owned by a publisher, so that that I can easily find one data package amongst all the data packages by this publisher.

column4: Researcher 1 distinguished local comparison of versions from understanding schema-change details.
column5: Researcher 2treated both stories as addressing version and schema change analysis.
column8: The final label is NONE because the first story concerns local comparison for analytics code, while the second concerns knowing detailed schema-change information.
column9: As a Data Analyst, I want to compare different versions of some datapackage locally, so that that I can see schema changes clearly and adjust my analytics code to the desired schema version.
column10: As a Consumer, I want to know full details of how the data package schema has changed, so that that I can adjust my scripts to handle it.

column4: Researcher 1 treated notification of changes and knowing what changed as part of the same change-awareness requirement.
column5: Researcher 2treated notification as a trigger that enables the consumer to inspect detailed changes afterward.
column8: The final label is DUPLICATE because both stories focus on consumer awareness of package changes and taking action based on those changes.
column9: As a Consumer, I want to know full details of how the data package schema has changed, so that that I can adjust my scripts to handle it.
column10: As a Consumer, I want to be notified of changes to a package I care about, so that that I can check out what has changed and take action.

column4: Researcher 1 treated viewing a publisher profile and searching packages by publisher as separate consumer tasks.
column5: Researcher 2treated searching among packages owned by a publisher as dependent on identifying or accessing the publisher context first.
column8: The final label is DEPENDS_ON because searching among packages owned by a publisher presupposes a publisher context, which is provided by the publisher profile.
column9: As a Consumer, I want to view a publisher's profile, so that that I can see who is behind a particular package or to see what other packages they produce.
column10: As a Consumer, I want to search among all data packages owned by a publisher, so that that I can easily find one data package amongst all the data packages by this publisher.
